topic_title: Spec-first discipline — writing the plain-English specification before writing or prompting code

required_sections.notes: All 4 required sections present and non-trivial. LOs: 5 well-formed bullets. Introduction: ~230 words with the 'how do you know if it's right?' motivation. Core Concepts: 5 subsections (5.1 what-is-a-spec table, 5.2 why-spec-first 4 reasons, 5.3 4-step workflow, 5.4 good-vs-bad spec comparison, 5.5 spec-as-prompt). Key Takeaways: 5 bullets.

citation_density.detail: All 5 primary resources cited. [1] Fowler cited in §4, §5.1, §5.2, §5.3, §5.4, §6, §7, §8, §10. [2] Joel on Software cited throughout all sections. [3] Prompting Guide cited in §4, §5.2, §5.3, §5.5, §6, §7, §8, §10. [4] Real Python comments cited in §5.2, §5.5, §10. [5] Anthropic prompt engineering cited in §4, §5.2, §5.3, §5.5, §6, §7, §8, §10.

soft_findings: §8 Best Practices: 'invalid input raises ValueError and is caught' — ValueError named as a constraint-language example, not taught. In-bounds: 11.5 deferred ValueError with context, this usage is similarly brief.
§7 Real-World Patterns: 'response must be JSON' — JSON named as a real-world AI pipeline constraint example only. Not a Python concept being taught.
sequence_drift.detail: Steps 3 and 4 of the workflow (explain every line / test edge cases) named-and-deferred to 11.8 and 11.9 explicitly: 'Steps 3 and 4 are introduced in 11.8 and 11.9 respectively.' No other forward references. All specification concepts introduced fresh in this topic.

length_sanity.detail: 2603 words — in standard range. 9 corpus sections. Good-vs-bad spec comparison and hands-on exercise add pedagogical weight appropriate for standard depth.

soft_findings: §5.3 introduces the 4-step workflow label including steps 3+4 by name ('Explain every line ← 11.8', 'Test edge cases ← 11.9') — labels named and explicitly deferred, not taught. Correct forward-pointer pattern.
scope_creep.detail: Core teaching stays within specification writing — what a spec is, why it comes first, how to write one, how to turn it into a prompt. No new Python syntax introduced. Steps 3 and 4 of the workflow are explicitly deferred.

beginner_readability.detail: Specification and all sub-terms (inputs/outputs/constraints/examples) defined on first use with a table. 4-step workflow uses a code-block format that is easy to scan. Good-vs-bad spec comparison makes the abstract concrete immediately. Grade-average script used as the running example throughout — familiar from 11.3–11.6.

verdict: PASS (cycle 1). 2603-word standard-depth corpus covers specification definition, why-spec-first (4 reasons), 4-step workflow (steps 3+4 deferred), good-vs-bad spec comparison, spec-as-prompt. All 5 primary resources cited. 3 soft findings (ValueError example, JSON example, step labels forward-pointed). No hard fails.
